perturbation.original_text: The confidentiality obligations c ontained in this Agreement expire and are of no further force or effect five (5) years from last date that Confidential Information was disclosed under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The confidentiality obligations contained in this Agreement expire and are of no further force or effect one (1) year from the last date that Confidential Information was disclosed under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality term to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets, especially in industries like vehicle development. California law provides broader protection for trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects trade secrets as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. A one-year confidentiality period may be deemed insufficient to protect trade secrets, thereby conflicting with the protections intended by CUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from five years to one year. The Perturbation Explanation states this may be insufficient to protect trade secrets, particularly concerning vehicle development. The Law Explanation reinforces this, stating a one-year period may conflict with CUTSA's intent to protect trade secrets as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) under Civil Code sections 3426, indicating that California law protects trade secrets. By shortening the confidentiality period, the modified contract weakens the protection of trade secrets, potentially conflicting with CUTSA.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall not disclose any Confidential Information to any third parties other than to its affiliates, and its and their respective officers, directors, employees, consultants or professional adviser s (collectively, “ Representatives ”) who have a need to know the Confidential Information for use in evaluating or pursuing a potential business relationship with Disclosing Party or its affiliates (“ Permitted Use. ”)
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient shall not disclose any Confidential Information to any third parties, including its affiliates, officers, directors, employees, consultants, or professional advisors, regardless of their need to know, unless required by a subpoena.
perturbation.explanation: This modification restricts the disclosure of confidential information even to internal personnel who require access, which might impede the Recipient's ability to comply with legal obligations such as reporting requirements under Sarbanes-Oxley.
perturbation.law_explanation: Sarbanes-Oxley requires companies to establish internal controls for financial reporting. By prohibiting disclosure even to internal staff who need the information for compliance, the modified clause could prevent the Recipient from meeting its obligations under Sarbanes-Oxley, particularly regarding accurate financial reporting and whistleblower protections.

Corporate responsibility for financial reports (a) Regulations required The Commission shall, by rule, require, for each company filing periodic reports under section 78m(a) or 78o(d) of this title , that the principal executive officer or officers and the principal financial officer or officers, or persons performing similar functions, certify in each annual or quarterly r
perturbation.justification: The modified text restricts access to confidential information even to internal personnel, potentially preventing the Recipient from fulfilling obligations under Sarbanes-Oxley, which requires internal controls for financial reporting as per 15 USC 7241. This statute emphasizes the corporate responsibility for financial reports, and the modified contract could impede the accurate creation and certification of these reports by limiting the necessary internal flow of information, creating a direct contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any waiver or failure to enforce any provision of this Agreement on one occasion will not be deemed a waiver of any other provision or of such provision on any other occasion.
perturbation.changed_text: Any waiver or failure to enforce any provision of this Agreement on one occasion will be deemed a permanent waiver of that provision.
perturbation.explanation: This modification implies that a single instance of waiving a contractual right constitutes a permanent abandonment of that right. This conflicts with the principle that waivers generally require intent and may be retracted under California law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 3268 allows individuals to waive the benefit of a law intended solely for their benefit, but case law requires a clear intention to waive the right. The modified text creates an automatic and potentially unintended permanent waiver, contradicting the requirement for intentionality as supported by case law interpreting this statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3268. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 16. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3268- 3268.] ( Title 16 enacted 1872. ) 3268. Except where it is otherwise declared, the provisions of the foregoing titles of this part, in respect to the rights and obligations of parties to contracts, are subordinate to the intention of the parties, when ascertained in the manner prescribed by the chapter on the interpretation of contracts; and the benefit thereof may be waived by any party entitled thereto, unless such waiver would be against public policy. (Amended by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text states that a single waiver is deemed a permanent waiver, which contradicts California Civil Code Section 3268. The law allows for waiver of rights but requires a clear intention to do so. The modified text creates an automatic and potentially unintended permanent waiver. Snippet 1 from California Code, CIV 3268 states that the benefit of the law may be waived, but this is subordinate to the intention of the parties. The changed text removes the element of intention, establishing a permanent waiver without regard to the party's intent, thus contradicting the law.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient acknowledges that its breach of this Agreement may cause irreparable damage to Disclosing Party and hereby agrees that, in addition to any other relief available at law or in equity, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to seek injunctive relief under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient acknowledges that its breach of this Agreement may cause irreparable damage to Disclosing Party; however, Disclosing Party waives its right to seek injunctive relief and is limited to monetary damages only.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting the disclosing party to only monetary damages may be insufficient to remedy a breach involving trade secrets or other confidential information, potentially conflicting with the remedies available under California's version of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 3426.2 specifically allows for injunctive relief to prevent threatened or actual misappropriation of trade secrets. Waiving the right to seek injunctive relief directly contradicts the remedies available under CUTSA, particularly in cases where monetary damages are inadequate to compensate for the loss of trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.2 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.2. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts California Civil Code Section 3426.2(a), as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states that "Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined." The modified contract explicitly waives the Disclosing Party's right to seek injunctive relief, limiting them to monetary damages only. This directly removes a remedy explicitly provided by law, thereby creating a legal contradiction and undermining the protection offered by the Uniform Trade Secrets Act.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and any action related thereto will be governed, controlled, interpreted, and defined by and under the laws of the State of California , without giving effect to any conflicts of laws principles that require the application of the law of a different state.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement and any action related thereto will be governed, controlled, interpreted, and defined by and under the laws of the State of Delaware, without giving effect to any conflicts of laws principles that require the application of the law of a different state. Any dispute arising under this agreement shall be resolved through mandatory binding arbitration in Delaware, with no right to discovery.
perturbation.explanation: For a California-based company dealing with confidential information related to vehicle prototypes, forcing arbitration in Delaware without discovery could conflict with California's interest in protecting trade secrets and ensuring fair access to evidence.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1281.85
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1281.85 generally permits provisional remedies, including discovery, in arbitration proceedings, especially when necessary to protect trade secrets. Prohibiting discovery in the arbitration clause undermines California's policy of allowing such remedies to protect trade secrets, potentially conflicting with the state's interest in fair dispute resolution and protection of proprietary information within its jurisdiction.

OF SPECIAL PROCEEDINGS OF A CIVIL NATURE [1063 - 1822.60] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 9. ARBITRATION [1280 - 1294.4] ( Title 9 repealed and added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) CHAPTER 2. Enforcement of Arbitration Agreements [1281 - 1281.99] ( Chapter 2 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) 1281.85. (a) Beginning July 1, 2002, a person serving as a neutral arbitrator pursuant to an arbitration agreement shall comply with the ethics standards for arbitrators adopted by the Judicial Council pursuant to this section. The Judicial Council shall adopt ethical standards for all neutral arbitrators effective July 1, 2002. These standards shall be consistent with the standards established for arbitrators in the judicial arbitration program and may expand but may not limit the disclosure and disqualification requirements established by this chapter. The standards shall address the disclosure of interests, relationships, or affiliations that may constitute conflicts of interest, including prior service as an arbitrator or other dispute resolution neutral entity, disqualifications, acceptance of gifts, and establishment of future professional relationships. (b) Subdivision (a) does not apply to an arbitration conducted pursuant to the terms of a public or
perturbation.justification: The changed text mandates arbitration in Delaware and prohibits discovery, which contradicts California's policy of allowing provisional remedies, including discovery, in arbitration proceedings to protect trade secrets (as explained in the Law Explanation). California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1281.85, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, indicates that arbitrators should comply with ethics standards adopted by the Judicial Council, suggesting a framework for fair arbitration that the changed text potentially undermines by denying discovery.
